title: Rally's ExtJS Grid Panel — Column Resize & Layout Internals
summary: Ext.grid.Panel column resize, flex layout, autoSize, and stateful persistence — applied to Vector's tree table.
content:
• 1. Overview
  • 2. Column Resize Internals
  • 3. Auto-Layout & Sizing Modes
  • 4. Persistence
  • 5. Lessons for Vector's Tree Table
  • 6. Sources
  • 7. Gaps

1. Overview

Ext.grid.Panel is the canonical grid widget in the Sencha ExtJS framework (Classic toolkit). Rally Software (now CA Agile Central) uses it as the foundation for all tabular data views in its web client — including the Portfolio Items, User Stories, and Work Items grids. The Rally App SDK 2.1 ships Rally.ui.grid.Grid and Rally.ui.grid.TreeGrid as thin wrappers that extend or compose Ext.grid.Panel, inheriting all column-layout, resize, and state machinery directly. [1][2]

Class hierarchy (Classic toolkit)

Ext.Base
  └─ Ext.Component
       └─ Ext.container.Container
            └─ Ext.panel.Panel
                 └─ Ext.panel.Table
                      └─ Ext.grid.Panel          ← the subject of this paper
                           ├─ Rally.ui.grid.Grid
                           └─ Rally.ui.grid.TreeGrid

The panel owns a docked Ext.grid.header.Container — an HBox layout container that renders and manages all column headers. The Ext.view.Table (the body) renders data rows and is kept in sync with the header container's column state. [3]

2. Column Resize Internals

2.1 The HeaderResizer plugin

Ext.grid.header.Container always includes Ext.grid.plugin.HeaderResizer as a required dependency. This plugin attaches a Ext.dd.DragTracker to the header container after the component renders (afterHeaderRender), wiring four handlers: [4]

HandlerPhaseBehaviour
onBeforeStartmousedownValidates an active resize zone exists; computes xDelta (offset between cursor and the column's right edge) to zero-calibrate subsequent drag calculations. Returns true to begin tracking.
onStartdrag beginsIn ghost (non-realtime) mode, positions left & right markers (lhsMarker / rhsMarker) at column boundaries so the user sees a vertical preview line.
onDragmousemoveIn realtime mode calls doResize() immediately; in ghost mode advances only rhsMarker to preview the new edge without touching DOM column widths.
onEndmouseupHides markers, restores overflow, calls doResize() to commit the final width, fires column-resize callbacks.

A cursor-probe listener (onHeaderCtMouseMove) runs on all header mousemove events — even outside an active drag — to detect whether the cursor is within the resize zone near a column edge, and sets cursor: col-resize on the container accordingly. [4]

2.2 The realtime flag (Classic: dynamic)

In the Classic toolkit the HeaderResizer plugin accepts a dynamic boolean (internally; surfaced as realtime in the Modern toolkit's Ext.grid.plugin.ColumnResizing): [4][5]

  • false (default in Classic) — Ghost-line mode. Only the right marker moves during drag; the column body does not reflow until mouseup. Lighter on the CPU for wide grids.
  • true / realtime: true (Modern default) — The entire column — header and all cells — resizes live on every mousemove via repeated setWidth() calls, triggering a full HBox layout pass per frame.

2.3 Flex columns during drag

When a user manually drags a flex column, doResize() executes delete dragHd.flex; before calling dragHd.setWidth(newWidth). This irreversibly converts the column from proportional to fixed width for the remainder of the session (unless state is restored on next load). [4]

2.4 forceFit: true — inverse neighbour adjustment

When the grid is configured with forceFit: true, every column implicitly receives a flex value managed by the HBox layout of the header container — there are no fixed-pixel columns. When the user drags a resize handle the plugin finds the next visible non-group column and applies the inverse delta: [4][6] 

// Simplified from HeaderResizer source
nextHd.setWidth(nextHd.getWidth() - offset[0]);

This keeps the total grid width constant (no horizontal scrollbar appears). A known side-effect is that forceFit can cause header and data cell widths to drift out of sync in some browser/Safari combinations, and its interaction with the state provider can produce undefined saved widths because flex values — not pixel values — are what the layout manages. [6][7]

2.5 Minimum width

The getConstrainRegion() method enforces a hard floor. The default minColWidth is 40 px; it is read from Ext.grid.plugin.HeaderResizer.minColWidth and applied to the drag constraint region. Locked-grid and shrink-wrap grids compute a modified constraint. [4]

3. Auto-Layout & Sizing Modes

3.1 Sizing mode reference

ConfigTypeDefaultBehaviour
widthNumber—Fixed pixel width; column never shrinks or grows with the container.
flexNumber—Proportional share of remaining container width after fixed columns are laid out. flex: 2 gets twice the space of flex: 1.
minWidthNumber40Drag floor enforced by HeaderResizer. Also prevents flex columns from becoming invisible.
maxWidthNumber—Upper drag cap; not enforced automatically by the layout — only by the resize plugin constraint.
defaultWidthNumber100Fallback applied by Ext.grid.header.Container when neither width nor flex is set on a column.

flex vs. forceFit: flex is a column-level config — only the columns carrying it participate in proportional layout. forceFit: true is a grid-level switch that makes the HBox layout treat all columns as flex items (overriding explicit pixel widths), ensuring no horizontal scrollbar regardless of column count. Group columns with a flex config internally propagate forceFit to their children. [6][8]

3.2 autoSize() method

Ext.grid.column.Column#autoSize() measures the rendered cell content in the current viewport and shrinks or expands the column to the longest content width found. A common override pattern triggers it on the store's datachanged event: [8][9] 

// From coding-ideas.de override pattern (ExtJS 6.5.3 Classic)
autoResizeColumns: function () {
    this.columns.forEach(function (column) {
        column.autoSize();   // removes flex — see caveat below
    });
}

Flex caveat: autoSize() calls setWidth() internally, which deletes the column's flex property. Any flex column that passes through autoSize() becomes a fixed-pixel column. Guard against this by checking column.flex before calling the method. [9]

3.3 Buffered rendering caveat

Buffered rendering (Ext.grid.plugin.BufferedRenderer) is enabled by default in ExtJS 6+ grids. It maintains only a small window of DOM rows: trailingBufferZone (default 10 rows) behind the scroll position and leadingBufferZone (default 20 rows) ahead of it. [10]

Because autoSize() measures only the cells currently in the DOM, it will under-size columns when the widest content is in a non-rendered row. Applications that call autoSize() on a buffered grid must either disable buffered rendering, pre-fetch all rows, or accept that the result is an approximation of visible-row content only. [10]

4. Persistence

4.1 Enabling stateful grids

Two configs are required: stateful: true and a stable stateId string. Auto-generated component IDs change across page loads and cannot reliably round-trip through the state provider — only a developer-supplied static string guarantees stable storage keys. [11][12] 

Ext.create('Ext.grid.Panel', {
    stateful: true,
    stateId: 'work-items-grid',
    columns: [
        { text: 'ID',      dataIndex: 'id',      stateId: 'col-id',      width: 90 },
        { text: 'Summary', dataIndex: 'summary', stateId: 'col-summary', flex: 1 }
    ],
    // ...
});

Each column must also have its own stateId for per-column width, order, and visibility to be restored correctly. [3][12]

4.2 What is persisted

PropertyPersisted?Notes
Column widths (px)YesSaved as fixed pixel values; flex is lost after first drag.
Column visibilityYesHidden columns remember their hidden state on reload.
Column orderYesReordering via drag-drop persists across page loads.
Sort stateYesActive sorters saved alongside column state.
Filter stateYesActive filters saved alongside column state.
Grid panel sizeYesWidth and height if initially configured and since changed.
Collapsed stateYesPanel collapsed/expanded status via Panel's getState override.
flex valuesNoConverted to px on first manual resize; original ratio not stored.
Scroll positionNoNot part of default getState() implementation.
Row expand stateNoTree node expand/collapse is not persisted by default.

4.3 State provider backends

ExtJS ships two providers: Ext.state.LocalStorageProvider (default for modern apps) and Ext.state.CookieProvider. Custom server-side providers implement the Ext.state.Provider interface to persist state to a REST endpoint. The provider must be registered globally before any stateful component initialises: [12][13] 

// In app launch()
Ext.state.Manager.setProvider(
    Ext.create('Ext.state.LocalStorageProvider')
);

4.4 forceFit + state provider interaction (known defect)

When forceFit: true, column widths are managed as flex fractions by the HBox layout — not as pixel values. The state provider attempts to save the pixel snapshot produced by getState(), but in some ExtJS versions this returns undefined for flex-managed columns because the resolved pixel width is only computed during layout, not stored on the config object. Workaround: avoid combining forceFit with stateful, or override getState() to read widths from el.getWidth() directly. [7]

5. Lessons for Vector's Tree Table

The relevant implementation is app/(user)/work-items/Example2Tree.tsx, specifically the useColumnResize hook (lines 113–163). Comparing its approach to ExtJS's internals yields the following recommendations:

  • Direct DOM mutation during drag is correct. Vector's hook already avoids setState during mousemove, mutating <col> elements directly and committing to React state only on mouseup. This mirrors ExtJS's own design: the ghost-line mode defers DOM column widths until onEnd, and even realtime mode batches through setWidth() rather than re-rendering the virtual DOM. The current pattern is sound — do not add React state writes to onMove.
  • Per-column minWidth floors are already in place but could be externalised. ExtJS reads minColWidth from the plugin config (default 40 px) and computes the drag constraint region once on drag start — it does not re-check on every frame. Vector's MIN_COL_WIDTHS ref follows the same pattern. Consider promoting the min-width array into the column definition objects so that adding or reordering columns cannot silently misalign the floor array.
  • Width persistence should use column-keyed storage, not index-keyed. ExtJS requires a per-column stateId so that the state key is stable even when column order changes. Vector's current approach saves the flat widths array by position — if a column is inserted or columns are reordered, the saved array will be applied to the wrong columns on the next load. Use column dataIndex or a stable slug as the localStorage key.
  • Avoid autoSize()-style DOM measurement with virtual/windowed lists. ExtJS's buffered renderer only renders ~30 rows at a time; autoSize() therefore measures only those rows. If Vector ever adds a "fit column to content" feature to Example2Tree, it must either walk all data in memory (no DOM measurement) or accept a visible-rows-only approximation — and must not call a size-measuring pass on a flex column without first recording and restoring the flex value.

6. Sources

  9. Coding-ideas.de — "Automatically resizing grid columns to the longest content width" — https://www.coding-ideas.de/2018/03/09/automatically-resizing-grid-columns-to-the-longest-content-width/

  13. Jorge Ramon — "Creating a Custom ExtJS State Provider" — https://jorgeramon.me/2015/creating-a-custom-extjs-state-provider/
  14. Sencha ExtJS 6.5.3 — Ext.grid.column.Column API reference — https://docs.sencha.com/extjs/6.5.3/classic/Ext.grid.column.Column.html

7. Gaps

  • The Rally primary URL (rally1.rallydev.com/docs/...) requires authentication; all Rally-specific details are inferred from the public Rally App SDK index page and the ExtJS documentation it cites as its upstream. Rally-specific overrides to resize or state behaviour are not verifiable without a live Rally account.
  • The exact ExtJS version shipped with Rally App SDK 2.1 is not stated in the public SDK index. Based on the API surface (HBox forceFit, bufferedRenderer default-true), it is consistent with ExtJS 6.x Classic, but the specific patch version could not be confirmed.
  • Sencha forum threads for the forceFit/stateProvider known defect (source 7) returned 403 during crawl; the defect description is reconstructed from the Sencha search snippet and the forum thread title.
  • columnresize event parameters (header ref, new width, old width) are referenced in ExtJS community posts but were not extractable from the truncated Sencha docs pages returned during this session.
  • The autoSize() internal implementation (specifically how it walks the cell elements) was not confirmed from source — the behaviour is described from secondary blog posts and forum discussion only.
